channel.title: The Indie Feed
channel.description: YouTube and web notes, distilled into a chronological digest.

item.title: The Pragmatic Engineer - Building OpenCode with Dax Raad
item.description: The rapid advancement of AI tools, particularly coding agents, has fundamentally altered the landscape of software development. While the promise of increased speed and efficiency is undeniable, the r...
item.content:encoded:
The Unseen Bottlenecks: Navigating AI's Impact on Engineering with Dax Raad

The rapid advancement of AI tools, particularly coding agents, has fundamentally altered the landscape of software development. While the promise of increased speed and efficiency is undeniable, the reality for many engineering teams is more complex. Dax Raad, co-founder of Open Code, a leading open-source coding harness, shares his insights on why simply adopting AI tools isn't a silver bullet for better software and how companies can navigate this evolving paradigm.

The Illusion of Speed

Despite the proliferation of AI-powered coding tools, Raad observes a peculiar paradox: "Objectively, stuff has become easier, but then why am I thinking as hard as I ever have?" He notes that while AI can accelerate the coding process, it doesn't inherently solve the fundamental challenges of product development. For companies in the pre-product-market fit stage, AI might help in "swinging a lot," but Raad emphasizes the greater value of "thinking a lot instead of swinging a lot."

Even for Open Code, which has achieved significant product-market fit, the challenge isn't a lack of features but an abundance of directions. "There's a million different directions we can go in," Raad explains. The ease with which AI can generate solutions can lead to a "Frankenstein" product if not managed carefully. Shipping too many features, even if individually useful, can result in a cohesive, unmanageable codebase. The core issue, Raad argues, is that "just because we can ship 10 times more doesn't mean we have 10 times as many good ideas to ship out there." This realization has led Open Code to focus on "how do I like slow everyone down?" and re-evaluate what aspects of traditional development processes still hold value.

From Minecraft Modding to Open Source Leadership

Raad's journey into technology began with a childhood fascination for programming, influenced by his software engineer father. His early forays included working on the modding framework for Minecraft, where he discovered a passion for creating "interesting sandboxes" and observing user behavior. This experience, coupled with interactions with experienced programmers in the modding community, laid a foundation for his future endeavors.

After founding a startup that was acquired and a subsequent stint at a healthcare tech company, Raad found himself drawn to open source. He joined the team behind SST (Serverless Stack), a toolkit for building full-stack applications. During this period, he also contributed to OpenNext, a project that addressed the need for deploying Next.js applications on AWS, highlighting his ability to identify and fill gaps in the developer ecosystem.

The Genesis of Open Code

The inspiration for Open Code emerged from a period of reflection after OpenCode's sister company, SST, achieved profitability. Raad and his co-founders recognized the transformative potential of AI in dev tools but also saw the "stupid" aspects of much of the market. They took "a few swings at a few ideas" before realizing that "cloud code was the first AI coding tool that stuck for us." This personal adoption led to the question: "Why weren't we the ones that built this?"

The strategic positioning of Open Code was key. Raad identified a significant gap: "no coding agent that was like, we are the open source option." This, combined with the intense competition among model providers, created a valuable niche. By establishing Open Code as the open-source alternative, they could leverage the competition to their advantage, a strategy that has proven highly effective.

Navigating Hypergrowth and Market Dynamics

Open Code's growth has been nothing short of explosive, reaching millions of monthly active users in less than a year. This rapid ascent, however, has come with its own set of challenges. Raad recounts the unexpected surge in January 2025, which was partly fueled by Anthropic's decision to ban subscriptions for their models within Open Code. This incident, while initially disruptive, inadvertently created a PR win for Open Code when OpenAI officially announced support shortly after.

This event exemplifies Raad's strategic approach: "pick one temporary bad guy and then galvanize all their competitors." This tactic allowed Open Code to rally industry support and solidify its position as a neutral platform. "If you get your positioning right, the world just keeps handing you wins that you didn't even expect," he notes.

The Business of Inference and GPU Constraints

The profitability of AI inference is a topic Raad finds particularly interesting. He explains that the cost of delivering a token is primarily driven by electricity and hardware, with significant margins possible, especially with open-source models. While large companies like OpenAI and Anthropic may enjoy even higher margins due to their scale and GPU deals, the underlying economics of inference are strong.

However, the industry faces a critical bottleneck: GPU supply. "The demand for inference is growing... but we haven't made our production of GPUs grow exponentially," Raad states. This scarcity leads to hoarding and intense competition for resources, impacting even companies of Open Code's size. Big tech companies, with their massive spending power, are vacuuming up available capacity, making it challenging for smaller players to secure the necessary hardware.

The "Muted Prickle" and the Erosion of Judgment

A significant concern for Raad is the impact of AI agents on engineering judgment. He describes the "muted prickle" that engineers used to feel when writing "hacky" code. This discomfort served as a feedback mechanism, encouraging more thoughtful design and refactoring. With AI agents, this feedback loop is disrupted. "The agent will just do the hacky thing for you," Raad observes, leading to a proliferation of suboptimal code without the engineer experiencing the immediate negative consequences. This can result in a codebase that is harder to maintain and refactor in the long run.

This observation led to a memo to his team outlining three key challenges: shipping features that aren't worth shipping, absorbing too many hacks, and neglecting code cleanup. The most concerning aspect, Raad admits, is that "I don't think we're trading this off to move faster. I think we're moving at a normal pace." The illusion of speed created by AI can mask a lack of genuine progress.

The Enduring Value of Quality and Taste

In an era where AI can rapidly generate code, Raad emphasizes that quality and "taste" remain crucial differentiators. He argues that while some companies may achieve success with "crappy products," this is not a sustainable model for building truly great software. "If you start to be lazy in one place, you start to become lazy everywhere. It's like an infection."

Raad admires companies like HashiCorp, led by Mitchell Hashimoto, for their commitment to building well-executed, open-source products that become industry standards. He believes that true quality stems from an irrational commitment to excellence, doing things that are not strictly necessary but contribute to a superior user experience. This commitment is what allows smaller companies to compete against larger, more heavily funded entities.

The Future of Engineering Leadership

The advent of AI is reshaping the role of engineering leadership. Raad notes a shift towards engineers focusing on "how to make it easy to ship code that is to safely ship code." This involves setting up guardrails, ensuring robust testing, and establishing clear patterns within the codebase to guide AI agents. While this might seem novel, Raad points out that it's essentially an amplification of age-old challenges: "How do we get a junior engineer to ship code safely without breaking stuff?"

The increased use of AI agents, which can be seen as "a bunch of idiots" working 24/7, necessitates stronger guardrails. This has led to a renewed appreciation for older patterns like Domain-Driven Design, which, despite their verbosity, promote modularity and safety. The key, Raad suggests, is to leverage AI to automate the tedious aspects of these patterns, allowing engineers to benefit from their structure without the manual overhead.

Key Takeaways

  • AI accelerates, but doesn't solve fundamental product challenges: While AI tools can speed up coding, they don't inherently improve strategic thinking or product design.
  • Beware the illusion of speed: Shipping more features with AI doesn't automatically mean faster progress or better products. Focus on shipping valuable, cohesive features.
  • Strategic positioning is crucial: Identifying and owning a unique market position, like Open Code's open-source stance, can be a powerful competitive advantage.
  • Quality and taste remain differentiators: In a crowded market, a commitment to building high-quality, well-designed products is essential for long-term success.
  • Feedback loops are vital: Maintaining direct feedback loops, both from users and within the codebase, is critical for sharp judgment and continuous improvement.
  • Embrace the "muted prickle": Engineers need to remain aware of the consequences of their code, even when AI agents are involved, to avoid a decline in judgment.
  • Adaptability is key: The role of engineers and engineering leaders is evolving, requiring a focus on safety, guardrails, and leveraging AI to amplify human capabilities.

item.title: The Artificial Intelligence Show Podcast - Ep. 216: Google I/O, Musk v. OpenAI Verdict, Andrej Karpathy Joins Anthropic & Meta Layoffs
item.description: The artificial intelligence landscape is rapidly evolving, with major players making significant announcements and legal battles shaping the industry's future. This week, Google I/O showcased a bold v...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Ep. 216: Google I/O, Musk v. OpenAI Verdict, Andrej Karpathy Joins Anthropic & Meta Layoffs

The AI Frontier: Google's Agentic Leap, Musk's Legal Woes, and Karpathy's New Chapter

The artificial intelligence landscape is rapidly evolving, with major players making significant announcements and legal battles shaping the industry's future. This week, Google I/O showcased a bold vision for an "agentic Gemini era," while the conclusion of the Musk v. OpenAI trial brought clarity to a high-profile legal dispute. Meanwhile, the AI community buzzed with the news of Andre Karpathy joining Anthropic, signaling a potential shift in the race for advanced AI capabilities.

Google I/O 2026: The Dawn of Agentic AI

Google's annual I/O conference marked a pivotal moment, with CEO Sundar Pichai heralding the "agentic Gemini era." This signifies a strategic shift from AI chatbots that merely answer questions to AI agents capable of taking action on behalf of users across Google's extensive product suite.

Key announcements included the general availability of Gemini 3.5 Flash, now the default model in the Gemini app and Google Search's AI mode. Google claims Flash outperforms previous generations on coding and agentic benchmarks and is significantly faster. Gemini 3.5 Pro is also rolling out to users, while Gemini Spark, a 24/7 personal agent deeply integrated with Gmail, Docs, Slides, and Calendar, is entering beta for Google AI Ultra subscribers. Spark demonstrated capabilities like parsing credit card statements for hidden subscriptions and monitoring emails for deadlines, with high-stakes actions requiring user confirmation.

Google also unveiled what it calls the "biggest upgrade to the search box in 25 years," transforming it into a dynamic field accepting text, images, files, videos, and Chrome tabs. The AI mode, now defaulting to Gemini 3.5 Flash, has surpassed one billion monthly active users. New information agents will monitor the web 24/7 for user-picked topics, reporting back to AI Pro and Ultra subscribers this summer.

On the media front, Gemini Omni, an "any input to any output" model with character consistency, physics accuracy, and conversational video editing, was launched. Google Pix, a new image tool built on Nano Banana 2, was also introduced. Notably, Omni Flash will be free within YouTube Shorts and YouTube Create.

The event also featured a demo of Agentic 2.0, Google's developer platform, which built a functional core operating system in approximately 12 hours using 93 parallel sub-agents for under $1,000 in compute. Closing the conference, Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis declared that Google is "at the foothills of the singularity," citing rising machine autonomy, working agents, code generation, and AI acceleration in science and math as evidence that the singularity era has already begun.

While the sheer volume of announcements was impressive, the complexity of Google's product portfolio continues to be a challenge for consumers to navigate. The company's vision for AGI and the singularity is ambitious, but the practical integration and understanding of these innovations for the average user remain a work in progress.

Musk v. OpenAI: The Verdict and Its Aftermath

The highly publicized lawsuit filed by Elon Musk against OpenAI and its leadership has concluded with a jury verdict rejecting Musk's claims. The judge dismissed Musk's $150 billion lawsuit, finding that he had failed to file within the legally required timeframe. Musk had accused OpenAI of breaching its founding nonprofit mission by pursuing a commercial path and accepting significant investment from Microsoft.

Neither Musk nor OpenAI CEO Sam Altman were present for the verdict. Musk's legal team indicated an appeal, while OpenAI expressed delight. The trial highlighted OpenAI's argument that Musk's suit stemmed from competitive sour grapes. Musk, however, criticized the judge and jury for ruling on a technicality rather than the merits of the case, stating it sets a dangerous precedent.

This verdict effectively removes a significant roadblock for OpenAI's anticipated IPO. While antitrust claims made by Musk against OpenAI and Microsoft remain, the judge has expressed skepticism regarding these.

Andre Karpathy Joins Anthropic: A Major Talent Acquisition

In a significant move for the AI research community, renowned AI researcher Andre Karpathy announced he is joining Anthropic. Karpathy, a founding member of OpenAI and former director of AI at Tesla, has been a prominent figure in the field, known for his work on large language models and his role in popularizing AI concepts through his educational content.

Karpathy will join Anthropic's pre-training team, focusing on large-scale training runs that imbue Claude with its core knowledge. Anthropic has stated that Karpathy will lead a new team dedicated to using Claude itself to accelerate pre-training research, a move that aligns with the industry-wide push to automate AI development.

His career trajectory includes a foundational stint at OpenAI, where he led the "World of Bits" project, an early attempt to teach neural networks to interact with the internet. After returning to OpenAI in 2023, he left again in February 2024 to pursue personal projects and educational ventures. His recent tweets, particularly those hinting at significant advancements in LLMs over the holiday break, are credited with sparking a surge of interest and investment in Anthropic. Karpathy's influence is so substantial that his pronouncements can move markets and shape industry narratives, making his move to Anthropic a noteworthy development.

Meta's Layoffs and the Shifting Workforce Landscape

Meta announced a significant restructuring, reassigning 7,000 employees into four new AI-focused organizations before laying off approximately 8,000 workers, representing about 10% of its workforce. This move signals Meta's aggressive pivot towards AI, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg betting the company's future on the technology. The company has also scaled back its metaverse initiatives and integrated AI into employee performance reviews.

The layoffs have been described as particularly difficult, with reports of low morale within the company. A leaked audio clip of Zuckerberg defending Meta's practice of recording employee keystrokes and mouse activity for AI training highlighted the tension between the company's AI ambitions and employee concerns. Zuckerberg stated that "AI models learn from watching really smart people do things," emphasizing the need for internal talent to train models more effectively.

A widely shared tweet from a San Francisco-based individual captured the broader societal impact of this AI-driven transformation. The tweet described a stark divide in outcomes, with a select group of AI professionals achieving immense wealth while many others feel their skills are becoming obsolete and their job security is threatened. This sentiment underscores a growing unease about the future of work, the perceived widening gap between the "haves" and "have-nots" in the tech industry, and the psychological toll of rapid societal change driven by AI.

Cloudflare and ClickUp: Proactive Restructuring in the Age of AI

Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince's op-ed in the Wall Street Journal shed light on the company's decision to lay off over 20% of its workforce, despite strong revenue growth. Prince argued that AI is fundamentally changing the nature of work, not by replacing "builders" or "sellers," but by increasingly taking over "measurer" roles—those in finance, legal, compliance, and middle management. He predicted this proactive restructuring around AI capabilities would become the norm.

Similarly, ClickUp's founder and CEO, Zeb Evans, announced a 22% headcount reduction, emphasizing that the business is stronger than ever. Evans framed this as a move towards a "100x organization," where AI enables a significant increase in output. He highlighted the emergence of "10x engineers" who direct AI agents rather than writing code themselves, with judgment being the key skill. Evans also noted that those who automate their jobs with AI will likely retain their roles as AI system owners or managers.

These actions suggest a fundamental shift in how businesses are structured and how employees are valued. The emphasis is moving towards individuals who can leverage AI to achieve unprecedented productivity, with companies like ClickUp even proposing significantly higher salary bands for those who demonstrate exceptional AI-driven impact. This proactive approach contrasts with the "AI washing" claims of the past, indicating a genuine strategic pivot driven by the perceived capabilities of AI.

The Growing Opposition to AI Data Centers

A recent Gallup poll revealed a significant public opposition to the construction of AI data centers, with 71% of Americans opposing such facilities in their local areas. This opposition is even stronger than that towards nuclear power plants, which have historically faced polarization.

Environmental concerns, including excessive resource use (water and energy) and pollution, are the primary drivers of this opposition. Quality of life issues like traffic and economic concerns such as higher utility bills also contribute. While supporters cite economic benefits and job creation, the data suggests that overcoming local opposition will be a major hurdle for the expansion of AI computing infrastructure. This issue is poised to become a significant factor in local and state elections.

The poll also indicated that younger generations, particularly Gen Z, are increasingly concerned about AI's impact on their future job prospects, skill erosion, and the trustworthiness of AI outputs and the institutions deploying them. While environmental concerns are significant, the data suggests a multifaceted animosity towards AI, encompassing job security, cognitive impact, and institutional distrust.

AI's Political Divide: Lobbying and Executive Orders

The political debate surrounding AI is becoming increasingly fractured. In New York, an AI industry pack backed by prominent venture capitalists and AI leaders is pouring money into a Democratic primary to defeat a state assemblyman who championed AI safety legislation. However, this same lawmaker has also received substantial support from employees at top AI labs and AI safety organizations, illustrating the internal divisions within the AI community.

Meanwhile, the Trump White House was reportedly preparing an executive order for a voluntary pre-release review process for frontier AI models. This initiative, intended to address national security concerns and maintain a competitive edge against China, faced last-minute lobbying from the tech industry, potentially derailing its implementation. The differing stances on AI governance among various political factions and industry players highlight the complexity of shaping AI policy.

Anthropic's Legal Battle with the Department of War Continues

Anthropic's legal challenge against the Department of War's designation of the company as a "supply chain risk" continues. The DC Circuit Court of Appeals heard arguments, with judges appearing divided. One judge expressed skepticism about the Department of War's claims, while others focused on the interpretation of the underlying statute and the court's reviewability. The outcome of this appeal remains uncertain, but it underscores the ongoing tension between AI companies and government oversight.

AI Use Case Spotlight: Enhancing Workflows and Decision-Making

This week's AI use case spotlight focused on practical applications within SmarterX. Mike Kaput shared his approach to restructuring his workday around AI capabilities, emphasizing the importance of single-tasking for deep work while offloading agent-executable tasks to run in the background. This iterative process involves building AI skills as needed and reviewing agent outputs during breaks.

Paul Ritzer highlighted his use of AI as a thought partner and advisor for a complex, long-term project involving finance, legal, and accounting. By leveraging AI's ability to quickly learn advanced topics and maintain context across conversations, he can prepare for meetings, ask informed questions, and generate necessary documents, effectively using AI as a sidekick for critical tasks.

AI Product and Funding Updates

The AI product and funding landscape remains dynamic:

  • OpenAI is reportedly preparing for an IPO, potentially valuing the company at over $1 trillion. They also announced that an internal reasoning model solved an 80-year-old math conjecture, validated by mathematicians. New enterprise offerings like "guaranteed capacity" aim to secure compute resources, and OpenAI is implementing C2PA conforming watermarking for generated images.
  • Anthropic disclosed a substantial $4.5 billion compute deal with SpaceX, which is preparing for a massive IPO.
  • Meter, an AI risk assessment nonprofit, published its first frontier risk report, highlighting potential risks like unauthorized deployments and deception by advanced AI agents from major labs.
  • Cloudflare evaluated Anthropic's unreleased Mythos model, finding it could chain low-severity bugs into high-severity exploits, raising concerns about the consistency of AI safety boundaries.
  • Nvidia reported strong Q1 FY27 earnings, with significant revenue growth driven by its data center business.
  • McKinsey announced the "Acorn Plan," an overhaul of partner compensation that shifts more towards equity, partly in response to AI's impact on consulting revenues and outcome-based pricing.

The week's developments underscore the rapid pace of AI innovation, the evolving legal and political landscape, and the profound impact AI is having on the workforce and society.

item.title: The Artificial Intelligence Show Podcast - Ep. 216: Google I/O, Musk v. OpenAI Verdict, Andrej Karpathy Joins Anthropic & Meta Layoffs
item.description: The past week has been a whirlwind of significant developments in the artificial intelligence landscape, from major product announcements at Google I/O to legal battles, high-profile hires, and substa...
item.content:encoded:
Google I/O, Musk v. OpenAI, Karpathy Joins Anthropic, and Meta Layoffs Dominate AI News

The past week has been a whirlwind of significant developments in the artificial intelligence landscape, from major product announcements at Google I/O to legal battles, high-profile hires, and substantial workforce adjustments. This episode of The Artificial Intelligence Show dives deep into these pivotal events, exploring their implications for the future of AI and business.

Google I/O 2026: The Agentic Gemini Era Begins

Google I/O marked a significant shift in Google's AI strategy, with CEO Sundar Pichai heralding the "agentic Gemini era." This signifies a move beyond chatbots that merely answer questions to AI agents capable of taking action on behalf of users across Google's vast product ecosystem.

Key announcements included:

  • Gemini 3.5 Flash: This new model, generally available on May 19, 2026, is now the default for the Gemini app and Google Search's AI mode. Google claims it outperforms previous generations on coding and agentic benchmarks and is significantly faster.
  • Gemini Spark: A 24/7 personal agent designed to integrate deeply with Gmail, Docs, Slides, and Calendar. Spark will enter beta next week for Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, demonstrating capabilities like parsing financial statements for hidden subscriptions and monitoring emails for deadlines, with high-stakes actions requiring user confirmation.
  • Google Search's Biggest Upgrade in 25 Years: The search box is being transformed into a dynamic field accepting text, images, files, videos, and Chrome tabs. AI mode, now defaulting to Gemini 3.5 Flash, has surpassed one billion monthly active users. New information agents will monitor the web 24/7 for user-selected topics.
  • Gemini Omni: An "any input to any output" model designed for character consistency, physics accuracy, and conversational video editing. Omni Flash will be integrated into YouTube Shorts and YouTube Create.
  • Anti-gravity 2.0: Google's agentic developer platform demonstrated its power by building a functional core operating system in approximately 12 hours using 93 parallel sub-agents for under $1,000 in compute.

Google DeepMind co-founder and CEO Demis Hassabis declared that Google is "at the foothills of the singularity," citing advancements in machine autonomy, working agents, code generation, and AI acceleration in science and math as evidence that this era has already begun.

While the innovation is impressive, the sheer volume and complexity of Google's product portfolio continue to pose a challenge for consumers to navigate. The company's aggressive push into agentic AI also raises questions about the future of organic search traffic and website engagement.

Musk v. OpenAI Verdict: Lawsuit Dismissed, IPO Roadblock Cleared

The highly publicized lawsuit filed by Elon Musk against OpenAI and its leadership has concluded with a jury verdict dismissing Musk's claims. The judge rejected the $150 billion lawsuit, finding that Musk had failed to file within the legally required timeframe.

Musk had accused OpenAI of betraying its non-profit mission by pursuing commercial interests and accepting significant investment from Microsoft. He sought substantial damages, the removal of Sam Altman from OpenAI's board, and a reversal of OpenAI's for-profit structure.

While Musk's legal team has indicated an appeal, the verdict removes a significant hurdle for OpenAI's anticipated initial public offering (IPO). The case also highlighted the intense competition and evolving strategies within the AI industry, with Musk himself pivoting XAI's focus and striking a substantial compute deal with Anthropic.

Andrej Karpathy Joins Anthropic: A Major Talent Acquisition

In a significant move for the AI research community, renowned AI researcher Andrej Karpathy announced he is joining Anthropic. Karpathy, a founding member of OpenAI and former director of AI at Tesla, is celebrated for his contributions to deep learning and his role in leading Tesla's self-driving programs.

Karpathy will join Anthropic's pre-training team, focusing on using Claude itself to accelerate research in this computationally intensive phase of frontier model development. His decision to join Anthropic, rather than starting his own venture or returning to OpenAI, underscores the company's growing influence and capabilities. Karpathy's previous work, including his "World of Bits" project and his influential educational content, has positioned him as a key figure shaping the discourse and direction of AI development. His move is expected to further bolster Anthropic's efforts in building advanced AI models.

Meta Layoffs: Restructuring Around AI

Meta has undergone a significant restructuring, reassigning thousands of employees into four new AI-focused organizations before laying off approximately 8,000 workers, representing about 10% of its workforce. This move signals Meta's strategic pivot towards AI, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg betting the company's future on its development.

The company is also implementing new internal policies, including recording employee keystrokes and mouse activity to train AI models, a practice that has generated controversy. The layoffs and restructuring reflect a broader trend across the tech industry as companies re-evaluate their workforces and priorities in the face of rapid AI advancements. The sentiment among remaining employees appears mixed, with some expressing concern about their roles and the company's direction.

Cloudflare and ClickUp CEOs on AI-Driven Workforce Changes

Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince has published an op-ed explaining the company's decision to lay off over 20% of its workforce, despite strong revenue growth. Prince argues that AI is fundamentally changing the nature of work, particularly impacting "measurer" roles (finance, legal, HR, middle management) rather than "builders" or "sellers." He predicts this trend will become the norm across the industry.

Similarly, ClickUp's CEO Zeb Evans announced a 22% headcount reduction, emphasizing that the business is stronger than ever and that the changes are proactive, aimed at restructuring for a "100x organization" that prioritizes output and embraces new ways of working enabled by AI. Both leaders highlight the need for employees to become AI-forward to remain relevant and valuable in the evolving job market. The concept of "10x engineers" who can orchestrate and direct AI agents, rather than solely writing code, is emerging as a key differentiator.

American Opposition to Data Centers and AI's Political Divide

A recent Gallup poll reveals significant public opposition to the construction of AI data centers, with 71% of Americans opposing them in their local areas, a figure higher than opposition to nuclear power plants. Environmental concerns, including excessive resource use (water and energy) and pollution, are the primary drivers of this opposition.

This sentiment is contributing to a growing political divide around AI. In New York, a state assemblyman championing AI safety legislation is facing opposition from a pro-AI industry pack, while also receiving support from AI company employees. Meanwhile, a proposed executive order from the Trump White House aimed at establishing a voluntary pre-release review process for frontier AI models appears to have been stalled due to lobbying efforts. These developments highlight the complex and often contradictory political landscape surrounding AI development and regulation.

Anthropic v. Department of War: Legal Battle Continues

Anthropic's legal challenge against the Department of War's designation of the company as a supply chain risk continues. The DC Circuit Court of Appeals heard oral arguments, with judges appearing divided. While one judge criticized the designation as an "overreach," others expressed skepticism about Anthropic's claims and the reviewability of the decision. The outcome of this appeal remains uncertain, but it underscores the ongoing tension between AI companies and government oversight.

AI Use Case Spotlight: Workflow Optimization and Thought Partnership

This week's AI use case spotlight focuses on practical applications:

  • Workflow Restructuring: One approach involves sorting daily tasks into two categories: those that can be delegated to AI agents and those requiring focused, single-tasking human cognition. This allows for background AI processing while the individual concentrates on deep work, with results reviewed during breaks. This methodology emphasizes the need for continuous iteration and skill development for AI agents.
  • AI as a Thought Partner: Another use case highlights AI's role as an advisor and thought partner, particularly for complex projects involving finance, legal, and operations. By leveraging AI with extensive context and knowledge bases, individuals can prepare for meetings, understand advanced topics in real-time, and generate follow-on documents and analysis, effectively creating a continuous collaborative process.

AI Product and Funding Updates

  • OpenAI: Is reportedly preparing for an IPO with a valuation potentially exceeding $1 trillion. The company also announced that an internal general-purpose reasoning model has solved an 80-year-old math conjecture. New enterprise offerings like "guaranteed capacity" are being introduced, and OpenAI is integrating C2PA conforming image generation with watermarking for provenance.
  • SpaceX: Filed for its IPO, potentially becoming the largest in history, with a significant compute deal with Anthropic disclosed.
  • Meter: Published its first frontier risk report, indicating that advanced AI agents could initiate unauthorized deployments and bypass security measures.
  • Cloudflare: Evaluated Anthropic's unreleased Mythos model, finding it capable of chaining bugs into high-severity exploits.
  • Nvidia: Reported strong Q1 FY27 earnings, with significant revenue growth driven by its data center segment.
  • McKinsey: Announced an overhaul of partner compensation, shifting more towards equity in response to AI and outcome-based pricing.

Key Takeaways

  • Google's Agentic AI Push: Google I/O signals a major strategic shift towards AI agents capable of taking action, impacting search and its entire product suite.
  • OpenAI's Legal Victory: The dismissal of Elon Musk's lawsuit clears a path for OpenAI's IPO, though legal battles and industry competition continue.
  • Talent Wars Intensify: Andrej Karpathy's move to Anthropic highlights the critical importance of top AI researchers and the fierce competition for talent.
  • AI-Driven Workforce Transformation: Companies like Meta, Cloudflare, and ClickUp are actively restructuring and reducing workforces, driven by AI's potential to automate roles, particularly in administrative and "measurer" functions.
  • Public Skepticism and Political Polarization: Opposition to data centers and a general distrust of AI institutions are growing, creating complex political challenges and debates.
  • The Double-Edged Sword of AI: While AI promises increased productivity and innovation, it also amplifies the impact of poor performance and raises concerns about job displacement, environmental impact, and the ethical deployment of advanced models.

item.title: The Pragmatic Engineer - Why Rust is different, with Alice Ryhl
item.description: 0:00 · What would your pitch be on why it's worth checking out Rust or working with Rust? You need a language that's reliable that's going to have as few bugs as possible. That's the idea of Rust. Wha...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Why Rust is different, with Alice Ryhl

Transcript

Intro

0:00 · What would your pitch be on why it's worth checking out Rust or working with Rust? You need a language that's reliable that's going to have as few bugs as possible. That's the idea of Rust. What is the ownership model?

0:11 · The idea that if you have a variable containing some object, then the object is only there. So, it's kind of exclusive. We talked about memory safety, but there is a keyword in Rust called unsafe. Generally, when unsafe is used, it's to add a new features to the language. As long as you design your API right, you can add new language features by using unsafe. What are additions?

0:32 · Additions are basically the way that Rust makes breaking changes without breaking people because they might change the syntax of the language. Do you use any of the AI tools for your day-to-day work contributing to Rust projects? So, I have been trying to use them. Honestly, Rust is quietly spreading as a language of choice to build reliable and performant applications. But what makes it different? Ellis Real is a software engineer working on Google's Android Rust team, a core maintainer of Tokyo, the de facto async runtime for Rust, and is a Rust language team adviser. In today's conversation, we cover the pitch on why Rust is worth to consider whether you're using TypeScript or C++ today.

1:15 · how concepts like ownership, the borrow checker, and the unsafe keyword work, and what are things that trip up newcomers to Rust, how the language is governed without a benevolent dictator, and how RFC's and additions work, and many more. If you want to understand what makes Rust different and why so many engineers say once it compiles, it works, this episode is for you. This episode is presented by Antithesis.

1:38 · Verify your systems correctness without human review or traditional integration test and avoid bugs or outages. This episode is brought to you by Sentry.

1:46 · Centry is application monitoring software built by developers for developers. The first time I used Sentry was 10 years ago back at Uber where Sentry helped keep us honest on when and where our services were breaking. I also use Sentry today to help me understand if the services and APIs I built for the pragmatic engineer are healthy or not.

35:55 · Can we talk about how the language is is built? Who builds Rust? What the process for for for doing it? How does it compare to a project like Linux? I know it's not a language, but but it's still a large open source project. I mean, it's really an open source project. I mean, you may know that MOS that it originally originates from Masilla, but it's not a Masilla project anymore at all. So today there's the rust project which has a bunch of different teams.

36:24 · There's the language team there's the which you know deals with the language.

36:28 · There's the library API team which deals which decides on the API of the standard library. And these teams kind of govern it. So it's the people who are these teams that run the language. And one interesting difference compared to some other popular languages like like Python or projects like like Linux is they have a benevolent dictator for life and Rust does not. How is this working and and how are decisions made when especially when they're contagious or when it could help for you know some just someone to just make a decision? Honestly, if the team doesn't sign off on it, it doesn't happen. I mean if something is really contentious contentious or really big it will probably end up being discussed at a conference for example. So there's a conference called Rust week where they have an event called the Rust all hands where they're basically taking all of the Rust developers and putting them in one place. So if there was a problem like that they would probably discuss it there.

37:27 · And how are these teams structured? So like you said there's a compiler language library and and dev tools at at the very least. How do they define the boundaries? Is it just a team kind of roughly defining them and then you just kind of agree? Because as I'm thinking at a corporate level like as as a company would run there's typically teams are are founded often top down leadership doesn't mandate this will be this team that team there's bottoms up happening but often times it's top down just because it's easier but here as I understand there is no top down it's it's people self-organizing yeah really when it comes to the design of the language the teams are really that's really it teams have been pretty good as far as I've seen at delegating So I've seen them sometimes say we think this is a lips API decision and we will go with whatever lips API decides.

38:18 · One thing that is interesting to talk about is the RFC process. So there's requests for for process. RFC request for comment is the way the ROS project makes big decisions but you know big ones is important to say. Basically the way it works is let's say you have a language feature that's kind of big. For example, I had one called derive smart pointer which basically makes some types in the standard library less special. So I mean sometimes people begin implementation already but the idea is that you write the RFC first.

38:54 · By the way that that's no different inside of like at U we had RFC process and same thing usually you follow it but sometimes you kind of start to build it and then just the idea is you write the the RC first. Yeah, I I know. It's just funny how it happens everywhere where there's engineers. I think it's is to build.

39:11 · You write this doc and it has a template and I think it's actually a pretty good template. The idea is the first section is I think motivation. Well, the first one is summary, but the first important one is motivation. So, you explain why this feature and then I think they have two really interesting sections. They have the guide level explanation and the reference level explanation. And what this is is that in the guide level explanation, you explain your feature as if you were writing a guide as if the feature already existed. And in the reference level explanation, you explain your feature again as if it already existed, but as if it would be in the language reference instead of a tutorial. This is really interesting.

39:54 · You know, Amazon does just related to this. When they ask people to build a feature, they have a press release. like imagine if you announced this. I just love how like both this and and what you're saying it forces you to just think from a very different perspective right from from how people will use this feature and then and then other sections there's uh I think you mentioned uh rationale alternatives prior art future possibilities yeah so that you know you get to explain so rational and alternatives I think is a pretty important section because you get to answer all of the questions before they get asked and you get to explain why did you pick this design and then some other design and I think that's usually a pretty big part of an IFC and then of course it's good to look at what did C++ do and what can we do in the future as well and once you have the RFC you send it out what happens then well somehow people you get people to look at it there is an RFC's repository and you can open a pull request there with your markdown document with RFC and people can discuss it you might also write up your doc somewhere else then we call it a prefc if it's not in the IFC directory but it's kind of the same thing. Let's say that the language team like it's a language team IFC and they're happy with it. So I said the IFC was how the language makes big decisions but then they actually use a process that comes up for all decisions essentially called the final comment period. And so the idea is they tell the bot to please start the approval process and then it will create a comment on GitHub with a checkbox for every team member. And then team members check their own checkbox. And once everybody from the team except for at most two have checked their checkbox then the final comment period will begin which is 2 weeks.

41:52 · Oh. So when it's only two check boxes left then two weeks starts. What what's the rational for this? This is very interesting. It is a historic fact more more like I mean you know let's say that everybody checks that box immediately and then the two other people didn't get to a chance to look yet. So it's to make sure that everybody has a chance to see it.

42:15 · Yes, I understand. And also to keep things moving at a sensible pace. So there's another part of the process which is concerns. Team members can file a concern which basically pauses the process until it's resolved and then once the two weeks pass it's accepted.

42:32 · So that's essentially how the team makes decision and this is not just IFC's. If you have I don't know a pull request changing something in the reference then the language team might say oh we need to make sure that everybody is on board with this change. they'll tell the bot to start an FCP on this random PR or some random issue or what wherever it might be. The same process applies and then once let's say it says lang language feature okay the RFC is accepted we know what we're going to build then the feature is just built you start to open your pull requests and get into a language pretty much but you you put the feature behind a feature flag in Rust we have this thing called nightly features which basically means that you can't use it normally but if you use the nightly build of the compiler You can once you have your you know your feature I mean you might begin but you know once you have your accepted RFC you start submitting pull requests uh you implement your feature it gets merged and people start using it experimentally on a nightly build. Yeah. And then at some point you might say okay I think this feature is ready and this is kind of a recent invention but we have come up with this idea of a stabilization report in the pull request that change that removes the feature flag. We write up a little report saying for example how it's been used and explaining like oh here are the dangerous edge cases here are the tests for each of the dangerous ed. So that kind of stuff and then they use the FCP process again to agree to stabilize it and then it's merged and so now you have a feature without a feature flag.

Building new features

44:18 · Yeah.

44:18 · In the main branch.

44:20 · Yes.

44:21 · Between 0 days and 6 weeks from that there will be a beta release of the compiler and it will be and it will be in it.

44:28 · Yeah. It will be in it and then 6 weeks later the beta release becomes the next stable release.

44:35 · Okay.

44:35 · So, so like Rust has a strict six week release cycle unlike with Linux. Well, Linux also has a a rough emo, but here you just take the state stable bash. You're not doing any special.

44:46 · Yeah. And so we don't really have a concept of beta features that are only available a available on beta.

44:52 · You you have the nightly pretty much nightly or people can get on to the latest branch which is not yet cut.

44:58 · Yeah.

44:58 · The main purpose of beta is to test like you get six week to test the stable release. So hopefully if there's any problems we can fix them before it goes out into a stable release.

45:09 · And you mentioned that RFC's are only for large decisions that need to be made and you've you haven't written that many either just because they're large. What about smaller decisions that you also want to get consensus on? So here there are a few different ways to do it. So let's say you want to add a new function in the standard library or a new type.

45:29 · Then the library team actually has this thing called an ACP an API change proposal which is basically just an issue on GitHub. So you open your issue, you describe your API, describe what the interface is and explain why you should have it. uh and it's much smaller than an RFC would be and then the library team can say this is okay with us and then you can implement it on unstable or nightly and then later you can then stabilize it and similarly the compiler team has it's kind of what major change proposals you you need you need to say how how it's called MCPs what is going to confuse a lot of people outside of we're not inside of Rust yeah it is what it is which for the small smaller features Yeah, even though it's called major, right? It's it's big enough that they need you to put up an issue, but not big enough to need an RFC. And for the compiler team, the classic example would be a new compiler flag. Now, Rust has new new builds every 6 weeks, right? But it also has additions. There's one in 2015, 2018, 2021, 2024.

Editions vs. versions

46:38 · What are additions? So the thing about additions that is different from versions is that if you're using version 1.90 of the compiler, everything is using that version. But different crates can use different editions. And so I might have a crate using the 2025 edition of the language and I can keep using that forever because Rust has a really really high backwards compatibility guarantee. So you write all of your code and the guarantee is that it's going to keep working forever.

47:14 · That's the idea anyway. Additions are basically the way that Rust makes breaking changes without breaking people because they might change the syntax of the language. For example, async a weight was added in addition and so code using the old addition can't use async await there. You could have a variable called async if you want. lead as evil 5 and then in the new edition you can't but you can still mix and match code written in different editions so they work together.

47:46 · Oh interesting.

47:47 · So I might have a library written in the 2021 edition and you can write your library in 2024 edition or your binary project and then you can still use my library. Why do you think Russ decided on additions which feels a bit more confusing to me as a developer as opposed to versions? Like when I look at Java's versions or PHP's versions or even Ruby on Rails versions, you know, there's always a m there's a major number. And I might be wrong, but it sounds to me that an addition is almost an equivalent of a of a major version at other languages. Is that putting it correctly or am I missing some details here? Really the big idea is to say we want the old code to continue working but we still want to change the language. And so the the difference from other kinds of versions of languages I mean Python 2 version Python 3 comes to mind is that you can totally mix and match in any way you want.

48:45 · You are a language team adviser uh or you recently became one. What is a language team adviser and and what do you do as part of this role? The language team advisor role is a way to be part of the language team light in some sense. So you're someone that they've said, "Okay, we trust this person's opinion, but you don't necessarily go to every single meeting." Like there's no checkbox for you on an FCP, though you can still file a concern. So yeah, it's really a way to participate in or help the language team without full membership. And full membership might entail a lot of things, right? For example, for me, it would entail going to meetings every Wednesday evening instead of like when I would normally have dinner. from your observation how what is the corporate influence like on on Rust in the sense that when I talk with with Greg KH he was telling me that in practice about 80% of the frequent Linux contributors are typically employed by a company which sees value in um adding their their features or or maintaining their features in in Linux which which turns out to be like a nice kind of I guess symbiosis in some ways for for Rust the the people working on it? Are they usually paid by their employers like you are? I I'm I'm sure there's people who are just every now and then contributing, but people who are spending more time on this. Do you see a pattern of corporations actually supporting this or is there a foundation that also supports people to work on this full-time or close to full-time? I would say here that the Linux kernel is truly unique in this particular aspect in that they have thousands of contributors doing it on work time. 54:28 · I guess. Well, not the same status, but but not not being experimental. It it clearly marks that it's more stable.

54:35 · Experimental sounds like it's it's not as stable. Basically, we've proved this is going to work. I mean based on what you've laid out with just with memory safety and of course being able to use unsafe when you need to that already like shows a lot of I guess promise plus there's there's some regulation as well right there's I think the US Department of Defense passed some regulations saying that they will not allow their agencies to use non-memory safe languages for the concern of security vulnerability. ilities and you know this would practically mean that Rust and other memory safe languages could be used but C, C++ or maybe one day systems that are built in this one might see larger scrutiny. Yeah, there have been different stuff like that from multiple different governments, right?

Does it sound like as we're talking about the RFC, for example, the RFC process, my sense was most of that was thinking about what to build, getting a consensus, making sure it's right, and it almost sounded like the actual code itself would be the I don't know, lesser. Of course, you know, there's a lot of work involved, but you see what I mean. the the actual time spent will probably be less than everything else around it.

57:02 · I think it's still an important part of the the process. I mean, a lot of people talk that about that when you use AI, it's really important to write tests and that kind of thing because then the AI is actually able to tell if it did it right. I think the same kind of principle applies with agents in that they can talk to the compiler. It will tell them what to fix. So I guess this could be a case. We we'll see. But Rust could be a pretty promising candidate for to use for agents because they can get more feedback and it's just hard it's harder to to ship certain type of bugs or maybe impossible to have certain type of bugs. Yeah, I think there is a aspect of that for places like the Linux kernel where I mean correctness is extremely important, reliability is very important. There's already multiple layers of human review which which is is in place. Do you see potential for AI generated code to prove helpful somewhere like actually truly helpful like again assuming that these things can generate you know as per specification or as per what what you need or or could could this be a place where it might be one of the places where we might not need that much because it it already feels a kernel to me it feels like a place where there's a lot of people coming in contributing and and there's there's a reason that change can propagate slower when When I was at the Linux kernel maintainer summit, one of the topics that came up was using AI for code review and there were some people there who had for example set up bots that would say when you send an email to the mailing list, it would feed it into an AI agent which would leave a review.

58:50 · That kind of stuff. And for example, Linos and others were talking about how these reviews were actually really impressive for kernel code. At least what's being discussed in the kernel community, that kind of use case seems like something people are excited about.

59:11 · And it sounds like this would not be a replacement obviously, but just one more way to get feedback, maybe doing it quicker and and just an additional safety net, if you will, right? I mean, that's the thing with memory safety, right? You make some sort of trivial mistake. It it's not some complex thing.
